entry.title: ABAP Performance Tuning in S/4HANA Series : Part 1 : Performance Tools
entry.content:
Stop Guessing, Start Profiling: Choosing the Right ABAP Performance Tool

Series: ABAP Performance Tuning in S/4HANA | Part 1 of 7 | Author: Manish Khanna

Your batch job missed its 3-hour window. Your Fiori app takes 12 seconds to open a purchase order. You open SE38, stare at the program, and start adding WRITE statements. This is the wrong approach, and it's costing your project days.

ABAP has a mature performance toolset. The problem is that most developers reach for the wrong tool first — or skip tooling entirely and guess. This series fixes that. Seven posts, each focused on one area, all grounded in real patterns from S/4HANA landscapes.

The Core Problem: Wrong Tool = Wasted Time

Every ABAP performance problem lives in one of three layers:

  1. Database — slow SQL, missing indexes, N+1 SELECTs
  2. ABAP runtime — CPU-heavy loops, string operations, object instantiation
  3. Memory — heap growth, object proliferation, static cache accumulation

There are also S/4HANA-specific layers:

  1. CDS / HANA — CDS view stacking, missing pushdown, unoptimized aggregations
  2. Code quality — anti-patterns catchable before you even run the program

The fastest fix is always in layer 1. A SELECT inside a loop touching 10,000 rows is worth 30 minutes of your time. A 2% ABAP CPU saving is not. Start at the database.

The Tool Landscape

 

Tool Transaction Primary Focus When to Use
ATCATC / ADTStatic code analysisPre-transport — catch anti-patterns before tracing
ST05ST05Database SQLSlow SQL, missing indexes, N+1 SELECT patterns
SATSATABAP CPU runtimeSlow logic, loop inefficiencies, method call overhead
ST12ST12ABAP + MemoryMemory leaks, object proliferation, OO-heavy programs
SM50SM50Live work processesProcess stuck right now, need immediate call stack
/SDF/SMON/SDF/SMONHistorical analysisIssue happened yesterday — need post-mortem call stack
SQL AnalyzerBAS / VS CodeHANA HEX queriesHANA execution plan, Why Not HEX analysis
DBACOCKPITDBACOCKPITDB monitoringHANA cache hit rates, expensive SQL history

Symptom-to-Tool Map

Use this before you do anything else. Match your symptom to the right tool.

 

Symptom Start HereThen
Slow report, unknown causeST05SAT
Long DB wait time in traceST05—
High CPU, low DB timeSATST12 if OO-heavy
Memory dump / STORAGE errorST12SAT
SELECT inside loop suspectedST05—
Fiori timeout / OData 504ST05 on OData backendCDS analysis
CDS view slowST05 + SQL AnalyzerEntity buffer review
Work process stuck (happening now)SM50 live call stackServer snapshot
Batch job was slow (yesterday)/SDF/SMON historical—
HANA aggregation query slowSQL Analyzer (HEX)Result cache
Pre-transport quality checkATC—

The Recommended Workflow

Follow this sequence for every performance engagement. Don't skip steps.

Step 0 — ATC: Run static analysis before any tracing. ATC catches SELECT *, SELECT in loop, missing FAE guards, and COMMIT WORK in loop in seconds. Fix these first — no trace needed.

Step 1 — ST05: Eliminate database problems. This is your highest ROI step. One missing index can cut runtime by 80%.

Step 2 — SAT: Profile ABAP runtime once SQL is clean. Find CPU hotspots, excessive loop iterations, method call overhead.

Step 3 — ST12: Add memory analysis if the program uses ABAP Objects extensively, or if you're seeing memory dumps.

Step 4 — CDS / HANA tuning: For S/4HANA workloads — entity buffers, result caches, HEX analysis.

⚠️Warning: Never run traces in production without authorization. Traces add overhead and may capture sensitive data. Use development or quality systems with representative data volumes — always.

 

 

What This Series Covers

 

Post Topic
Part 1 (this post)Tool selection: which tool for which symptom
Part 2ST05: killing N+1 queries and full table scans
Part 3SAT + ST12: CPU profiling and memory leak detection
Part 4ATC: pre-transport quality gate and CI/CD integration
Part 5S/4HANA: CDS pushdown, entity buffers, result caches
Part 6Production incidents: call stacks, /SDF/SMON, parallel batch
Part 7Complete anti-pattern catalogue (DB · ABAP · OO · CDS · HANA)

Start from the top or jump to the symptom you're facing right now. Each post stands alone.

Key Takeaways

  • Match your symptom to the right tool before you open any code
  • ST05 first — database problems have the highest ROI fix
  • ATC before any trace — it catches the most common anti-patterns for free
  • Never trace in production; never guess without a tool

Next in this series: The ST05 Playbook: Killing N+1 Queries and Full Table Scans in ABAP — the step-by-step guide to SQL trace analysis with real code fixes.

Series Home Page

entry.title: IBP Cloud with multiple ERP using Real-Time Integration
entry.content:
• Overview
  • Target Audience
  • Business Scenarios
  • Scenario 1:Adding an Additional ERP to Existing Planning Area
  • Scenario 2: Additional Planning Area in IBP with one or multiple ERP systems
  • Core Concepts and Terminology
  • Configuration Guide by Scenario
  • Scenario 1: Adding an Additional ERP to Existing Planning Area
  • IBP Inbound Configuration
  • IBP Outbound Configuration
  • Scenario 2: Creating a New IBP Planning Area with Dedicated ERP Data Source(s)
  • IBP Inbound Configuration
  • IBP Outbound Configuration
  • Queue Prefix Strategy
  • Conclusion

Overview

SAP IBP Real-Time Integration (RTI) enables near-real-time, bidirectional replication of data between one or more ERP systems and IBP Cloud. This capability is essential for organizations requiring synchronized master data, transactional data, and planning parameters across distributed enterprise planning landscapes.

While initial Real-Time integration setup follows a well-defined procedure with established options—such as the CIAS guided workflow (strongly recommended for first-time implementations) or documented screenshot guides—this article focuses on extending existing RTI infrastructures with additional ERP systems or IBP planning areas.

 

Target Audience

This article is intended for:

  • Technical Project Leads overseeing landscape extensions and data synchronization strategies
  • IBP Integration Architects responsible for designing multi-system landscapes
  • IBP Consultants implementing and maintaining real-time integration solutions
  • SAP BASIS/ERP Administrators managing on-premise connectivity infrastructure

 

Business Scenarios

This guide addresses two primary scenarios requiring careful configuration:

Scenario 1: Adding an Additional ERP to Existing Planning Area

  • Existing Planning Area, RTI Profile and ERP connectivity
  • New ERP system to be connected
  • Requirement for bidirectional integration (inbound and outbound)

Key Consideration: data from additional ERP system can be integrated into the same Planning Area only if the CIF framework is configured with the same CIF LOGSYS name. This ensures data consolidation from multiple source systems into a single planning context.

Scenario 2: Additional Planning Area in IBP with one or multiple ERP systems

Prerequisites:

  • Existing Planning Area, RTI Profile and ERP connectivity
  • One or more new ERP systems 
  • Requirement for isolated or specialized planning logic

Key Consideration: A new, uniquely-named CIF LOGSYS must be created, distinct from existing identifiers, to establish a separate integration route.

In both cases there are similar steps that have to be performed, however there are slight differences as well that I will detail out in the next sections.

 

Core Concepts and Terminology

Before addressing configuration, understanding the following key concepts is essential:

CIF Logsys
The unique (10 chars long) identifier that defines an integration route from an on-premise ERP system to an IBP planning Area. We usually use it in the following transactions: BD54, NDV2, CFC1, CFC91, SM59, CFI1

Real-Time Integration Profile
A configuration object that establishes the connection between:

  • A specific IBP Planning Area (data target)
  • A specific CIF LOGSYS value (integration route). In the Real-Time integration profile app this field is called "IBP Logical System".
  • Mapping and transformation rules for incoming data
  • Settings for outgoing data

Critical Rule: A CIF LOGSYS value uniquely determines the destination Planning Area. Incoming RTI messages contain the CIF LOGSYS identifier, which the system uses to look up the corresponding RTI Profile and route data accordingly.

Planning Area
The IBP object serving as the final data repository, encompassing:

  • Master data (materials, locations, suppliers)
  • Key figures (demand, supply, inventory)
  • Planning levels and hierarchies
  • Time profiles and calendars

 

Configuration Guide by Scenario

Scenario 1: Adding an Additional ERP to Existing Planning Area

Pre-Configuration Checklist

  • Determine the target Planning Area
  • Check the linked RTI Profile and note the CIF LOGSYS, this will be needed in the configuration steps

 

IBP Inbound Configuration

Step 1: Communication Arrangements (IBP)
  • SAP_COM_0805 - No action required. This is a technical communication setup between IBP Cloud and BTP which can be reused without modification for this scenario.
  • SAP_COM_0769  - used for transactional data integration from ERP to IBP.  It must be extended with the new logsys of the new ERP system. 
  • SAP_COM_0770 - used for master data integration from ERP to IBP. It must be extended with the new logsys of the new ERP system

For logsys determination of the source system and client you can use T000 table in source system. Add that value into the below multi value field:

If you want to have dedicated technical for each ERP -> IBP connection:

  1. Create new Communication User in IBP
  2. assign the new Communication User to a new or existing (used by existing SAP_COM_0769 or 0770) Communication System
  3. Create new Communication Arrangements inherited from SAP_COM_0769 and 0770 and assign the logsys of the source system to it and select the new Communication User for Inbound Communication section
Step 2: Cloud Connector (On-Premise Infrastructure)
  • On-Premise to Cloud settings: No additional configuration required. The existing service channel to IBP Cloud remains active and can handle traffic from multiple ERPs simultaneously.
Step 3: ERP-Side CIF Configuration (in the new ERP)
  • Configure CIF using the CIF LOGSYS you noted down as part of the pre-configuration checklist (BD54 | NDV2 | CFC1 | SM59 | CFC1).
    • Follow chapters 5.3, 5.4, 5.5 in screenshot guide
    • In SM59 use either the existing Inbound Communication user or the new one depending on how you performed the configuration in Step 1.
  • Make sure that connection is working (SM59 authorization test)
Step 4: RTI Profile Update (IBP)

Modify the existing RTI Profile:

  • Inbound Systems section:  Add the new ERP's logsys identifier

(Optional: BADI Enhancement)
  • If the source ERP's data requires transformation or enrichment (e.g., adding prefixes/suffixes to distinguish between source systems):
  • Implement BADI (Business Add-In) in the ERP system, use the BADI to intercept and modify CIF outbound messages before transmission. Common use cases:
    • Prefixing material codes (`ERP1_MAT001`)
    • Filtering sensitive data or consolidating duplicate records

 

IBP Outbound Configuration

Step 1: ERP-Side Technical User Setup
  • Create two dedicated technical users in the new ERP system:
    • Configuration User: technical user used for Initial configuration data pull (Calendar, UoM, Currency)
    • Transactional User: technical user used for planning data integration to ERP
  • Make sure that PFCG roles properly assigned
  • Follow chapters 6.1, 6.2 in screenshot guide
Step 2: Cloud Connector Mapping
  • Create a new Cloud-to-On-Premise mapping specific to the additional ERP
  • assign RFC resources to the new mapping rule
  • Follow chapters 4.2 and 4.4 in screenshot guide. If you have multiple application servers in ERP side (Load Balancing case, use Chapter 4.3 instead of 4.2)
Step 3: Communication Arrangements (IBP)

Create new Communication Arrangements for outbound data delivery:

  • SAP_COM_0574 - No action required. This is a technical communication setup between IBP Cloud and BTP.  in case of landscape extension with additional ERP system, this communication will be reused automatically.
  • SAP_COM_0684 - Create new specific to the target ERP.  Use the cloud connection mapping created in the previous step.
  • SAP_COM_0685 - Create new specific to the target ERP. Use the cloud connection mapping created in the previous step.
  • Follow chapters 6.3 and 6.4 in screenshot guide. 

Step 4: RTI Profile - Update Outbound Systems (IBP)

  • Open the RTI Profile linked to the target Planning Area
  • Add the new ERP's logsys to the Outbound Systems list

Important: If this is the first time the Outbound Systems list contains more than one entry, you must define distribution logic for all outbound logsys entries otherwise existing integrations will not work. 

Step 5: BGRFC Inbound configuration (ERP)

When you setup an ERP system the first time for IBP Real-Time integration, the bgrfc framework has to be prepared

  • bgrfc scheduler configuration
  • Inbound destination for messages that starts with specific queue prefix.
  • Follow chapters 6.5 in screenshot guide. 

 

Scenario 2: Creating a New IBP Planning Area with Dedicated ERP Data Source(s)

Pre-Configuration Checklist

  • Determine the new Planning Area (master data, key figures, hierarchies)
  • New RTI Profile should be created to the new Planning Area
  • Define a new CIF LOGSYS which was never used before

IBP Inbound Configuration

Step 1: Communication Arrangements (IBP)
  • SAP_COM_0805 - No action required. This is a technical communication setup between IBP Cloud and BTP.  in case of landscape extension with additional ERP system, this communication will be reused automatically.
  • SAP_COM_0769  - used for master data integration from ERP to IBP.  It must be extended with the new logsys of the new ERP system
  • SAP_COM_0770 - used for transactional data integration from ERP to IBP. It must be extended with the new logsys of the new ERP system

If you want to have dedicated technical for each ERP -> IBP connection:

  1. Create new Communication User in IBP
  2. assign the new Communication User to a new or existing (used by existing SAP_COM_0769 or 0770) Communication System
  3. Create new Communication Arrangements inherited from SAP_COM_0769 and 0770 and assign the logsys of the source system to it and select the new Communication User for Inbound Communication section
Step 2: Cloud Connector
  • On-Premise to Cloud settings: No additional configuration required. The existing service channel to IBP Cloud remains active and can handle traffic from multiple ERPs simultaneously.
Step 3: ERP-Side Configuration
  • Configure CIF using the CIF LOGSYS you noted down as part of the pre-configuration checklist (BD54 | NDV2 | CFC1 | SM59 | CFC1).
    • Follow chapters 5.3, 5.4, 5.5 in screenshot guide
    • In SM59 use either the existing Inbound Communication user or the new one depending on how you performed the configuration in Step 1.
  • Make sure that connection is working (SM59 authorization test
Step 4: IBP Configuration

Create new objects:

  • New Planning Area:
    • Define master data dimensions (materials, locations, suppliers, etc.)
    • Configure key figures (forecasts, safety stock, demand patterns)
    • Establish planning levels and aggregation hierarchies
    • Set time profiles (daily, weekly, monthly planning buckets)
  • New RTI Profile:
    • IBP logsys: must match the value of the new CIF LOGSYS created in Step 3
    • Inbound Systems: Add the source ERP's LOGSYS
    • Mapping rules: Define field-to-field transformations from ERP to IBP

 

IBP Outbound Configuration

Step 1: ERP-Side Technical Users
  • Create two dedicated technical users in the new ERP system:
    • Configuration User: technical user used for Initial configuration data pull (Calendar, UoM, Currency)
    • Transactional User: technical user used for planning data integration to ERP
  • Make sure that PFCG roles properly assigned
  • Follow chapters 6.1, 6.2 in screenshot guide
Step 2: Cloud Connector Mapping
  • Create a new Cloud-to-On-Premise mapping specific to the additional ERP
  • assign RFC resources to the new mapping rule
  • Follow chapters 4.2 and 4.4 in screenshot guide. If you have multiple application servers in ERP side (Load Balancing case, use Chapter 4.3 instead of 4.2)
Step 3: Communication Arrangements (IBP)

Create new Communication Arrangements for outbound data delivery:

  • SAP_COM_0574 - No action required. This is a technical communication setup between IBP Cloud and BTP.  in case of landscape extension with additional ERP system, this communication will be reused automatically.
  • SAP_COM_0684 - Create new specific to the target ERP.  Use the cloud connection mapping created in the previous step.
  • SAP_COM_0685 - Create new specific to the target ERP. Use the cloud connection mapping created in the previous step.
  • Follow chapters 6.3 and 6.4 in screenshot guide. 
Step 4: RTI Profile - Update Outbound Systems (IBP)
  • Open the RTI Profile linked to the target Planning Area
  • Add the new ERP's logsys to the Outbound Systems list

Important: If this is the first time the Outbound Systems list contains more than one entry, you must define distribution logic for all outbound logsys entries otherwise IBP outbound integration will not work. 

Step 5: BGRFC Inbound configuration (ERP)

When you setup an ERP system the first time for IBP Real-Time integration, the bgrfc framework has to be prepared

  • bgrfc scheduler configuration
  • Inbound destination for messages that starts with specific queue prefix.
  • Follow chapters 6.5 in screenshot guide. 

Queue Prefix Strategy

If you integrate from multiple ERP systems to an IBP Cloud system it is useful to separate the queues by CIF LOGSYS. It has the following benefits:

  • Enables queue-level monitoring and alerting
  • Simplifies debugging by isolating traffic for each integration route

Conclusion

Extending SAP IBP landscape to support multiple ERP systems with Real-Time integration capabilities requires methodical planning and precise configuration. The distinction between consolidating data into a single Planning Area (Scenario 1) versus creating specialized Planning Areas with separate integration routes (Scenario 2) fundamentally determines the CIF LOGSYS naming, RTI Profile structure, and distribution logic.

By adhering to these guidelines, organizations can build scalable, maintainable multi-system planning landscapes that deliver real-time data synchronization and support complex business processes across distributed enterprises.

entry.title: Orchestrating AI Assisted HR Workflows with Microsoft Copilot Using SAP BTP and SuccessFactors
entry.content:
Employees increasingly expect quick answers to questions such as:

  • What is the status of my promotion request

  • Which onboarding tasks are still pending for new hires

The objective was to allow AI-assisted HR queries while maintaining strict governance over SAP SuccessFactors data and workflows.

The organization had very clear requirements. AI tools should not have direct access to SuccessFactors APIs, and they should not execute actions using external tokens. All interactions had to remain fully controlled, auditable, and aligned with HR governance policies.

To achieve this, we designed an architecture where SAP BTP served as the orchestration and control layer between Microsoft Copilot and SuccessFactors.

When a user interacts with Copilot and asks a question related to HR processes, the request is not sent directly to SuccessFactors. Instead, it is routed to SAP Integration Suite running on SAP BTP.

At this stage, the integration layer performs several important functions. The request is validated, enriched with user context, and evaluated against access policies. This ensures that the request complies with the organization’s HR security and authorization rules.

Once validated, the request triggers a workflow through SAP Build Process Automation. This workflow layer applies business rules and approval logic related to HR processes. For example, certain HR data may only be accessible to managers or HR partners, and some actions may require additional validation steps.

Only after these governance checks are completed does the integration layer interact with SAP SuccessFactors modules such as Employee Central, onboarding workflows, or performance management services.

The requested information is retrieved in a controlled manner and transformed into a simplified response that can be returned to the AI interface.

From the user’s perspective, Copilot appears to instantly provide HR insights. However, behind the scenes the request has passed through a fully governed integration and workflow orchestration layer within SAP BTP.

This approach provided several advantages for the organization. AI could enhance the employee experience without introducing uncontrolled access to HR systems. All interactions remained traceable and compliant with internal policies. At the same time, the architecture created a scalable foundation for future AI-driven HR scenarios.

As enterprises continue integrating AI assistants into workplace tools, the key challenge will not be enabling AI itself but ensuring it operates within controlled enterprise architectures. Platforms such as SAP BTP provide the necessary capabilities to bridge modern AI services with critical business applications while maintaining governance, security, and operational visibility.
